Rhythmic Devices
Notes and rests have durations extended by ties and augmentation dots.
Pitches are discrete frequencies notated on staffs with clefs; octaves and accidentals distinguish pitch.
Half steps and whole steps form fundamental pitch patterns and interval structures.
Half and whole step patterns create major and minor scales.
Music is in a key when scale pitches predominate with that pitch as center.
Meter consists of three interlocking pulse speeds: beat, beat division, and measure.
Meter types determined by beat-division relationship and beat-measure grouping patterns.
Parallel keys share the same tonic but have different key signatures.
Rhythmic patterns derive from beat divisions and can be identified, notated, and performed.
Tempo markings express relative speed using Italian words or metronome markings.
Dynamics indicate relative loudness using Italian abbreviations, hairpins, and accent symbols.
Minor scales use specific half/whole step patterns; harmonic and melodic forms alter natural minor.
Relative keys share key signatures but have different tonics.
Chromatic, whole-tone, and pentatonic scales provide alternative pitch organization systems used in melodies.
Intervals measured by distance and quality; enharmonic equivalents sound identical but differ in spelling.
Interval inversion found by moving lower note up octave; inversions sum to nine.
Convert notated pitches to sounding pitches for transposing instruments using provided specifications.
Standard ensembles and unique mixes identified by distinctive instrument/voice timbres and registers.
Melody combines pitch and rhythm into expressive pitch succession.
Moving melody to new pitch level while preserving intervals and rhythm.
Texture combines simultaneous musical components influenced by timbre, pitch density, and range.
Texture descriptions include specific devices like Alberti bass, canon, and ostinato.
Tonal music organizes pitches hierarchically around a central tonic pitch in common practice tradition.
Syncopation, cross-rhythm, and hemiola challenge metric regularity through accentuation and rhythmic contrast.
Chords contain three or more pitches simultaneously or arpeggiated; triads and seventh chords are stacked in thirds.
Diatonic chords identified by Roman numerals indicating scale degree, quality, and function.
Chords arranged with different bass notes create inversions labeled by figured bass numerals.
Seventh chords commonly exhibit major, minor, or fully-diminished quality.
Seventh chords can be inverted with the seventh degree in bass.
Musical lines classified as soprano, alto, tenor, bass based on pitch position.
Follow standard conventions when writing chordal sevenths in four-voice texture.
Inverted seventh chords enable stepwise bass motion and smooth voice leading.
Predominant chords intensify key through tonic-predominant-dominant-tonic progressions.
Mediant triads rarely appear in common practice harmony; III in minor represents relative major.
Plagal and Phrygian half cadences use predominant function; deceptive cadences avoid authentic V-I resolution.
Second-inversion triads appear only in four specific harmonic contexts.
Pedal 6/4 chord embellishes triad thirds and fifths with neighbor tones, static bass.
Unaccented passing and neighbor tones enliven bass lines in chorale style.
Nonharmonic tones include anticipations, escape tones, appoggiaturas, and pedal points.
Motives are short melodic/rhythmic units varied through transformation procedures.
Melodic sequence repeats a segment at consistent transposition intervals.
Repeated chord segment transposed at consistent intervals; may accompany melodic sequence.
Tonicization temporarily emphasizes non-tonic chords through altered scale degrees shown as accidentals.
Secondary dominants follow same doubling and voice-leading rules as regular dominants.
Secondary leading-tone chords tonicize major or minor triads; triads appear inverted, sevenths in any inversion.
Secondary leading-tone chords follow standard doubling and voice-leading rules.
Seven modal scales (Ionian through Locrian) are foundational pitch categories in music theory.
Phrases use similarity or contrast represented by lowercase letters: a a' or a b.
Periods combine antecedent and consequent phrases; parallel or contrasting types.
Chromatic bass pitches imply tonicization; ♯4 resolving to 5 suggests Ⅴ/Ⅴ.
Agogic accent, anacrusis, and fermata extend or alter normal rhythmic duration and timing.
Composers transform motives through procedures affecting rhythm, pitch, or both combined.
Chord members in stacked-thirds configuration are named: root, third, fifth, and seventh.
Cadential 6/4 embellishes dominant, occupies strong metrical position before it.
Harmonic progression is a chord sequence represented by Roman numerals with Arabic numerals for inversions.
Figured bass numerals indicate intervals above the bass note, implying specific harmonic chords.
Chord members with resolution tendency, like sevenths, function as chordal dissonance.
Aural exam assessment requires relative pitch identification with reference pitches provided.
Passing 6/4 harmonizes scale fragment; upper voices move stepwise on weak beat.
Scale degrees function relative to tonic using names or numbers.
All chords must be spelled correctly with required accidentals in progressions.
Key signatures show scale pitches; diatonic pitches belong to key, chromatic pitches do not.
Simple meter divides the beat into two; compound meter divides it into three.
Time signature numerators indicate meter type; denominators indicate beat or division value.
Rhythmic notation must clearly show beat structure through proper beaming and sequencing.
Tempo adjustments include accelerando, ritardando, ritenuto, and rubato for flexibility.
Articulation describes attack, release, and pitch connection using symbols, words, and interpretive flexibility.
Minor keys share key signatures with their relative major keys.
Closely related keys differ by no more than one accidental; their tonics are diatonic triads.
Approach sevenths by common tone or step; alternatively by ascending leap or descending third leap.
Harmonic intervals are simultaneous; melodic intervals are successive; steps and leaps categorize melodic intervals.
Compound intervals are simple intervals plus octave; they sound similar but larger.
Western instrumental families identified by timbre and register; specialized ensembles and groupings.
Leading-tone seventh chords function as dominant substitutes or tonic prolongations.
Melodies derive from scales/modes organized as motives and phrases.
Secondary dominant chords precede major or minor triads, creating temporary tonics through V-I progression.
Main texture types: monophony, homophony, polyphony, heterophony; includes counterpoint and canonic forms.
Subdominant and supertonic chords precede dominant and function as predominant harmonies.
Doubling conventions: prefer roots, allow thirds/fifths, never sevenths, special rules for V7 and 6/4.
Spell inverted seventh chords completely; resolve tendency tones individually.
Slashes and accidentals in figured bass indicate chromatic alterations to specific pitches.
Rhythmic patterns transform through augmentation and diminution procedures.
Sixth and fourth above bass resolve downward by step in cadential 6/4.
Major and minor keys are called modes; shifting between them changes mode.
Arpeggiated 6/4 from bass triad arpeggio; upper voices static, waltz-like pattern.
Distantly related keys differ by more than one accidental; includes parallel keys.
Performed rhythms should match notation unless style allows deviation, like swing.
The circle of fifths visually displays relationships and proximity among keys.
Metrical accents follow strong-weak patterns; downbeat strongest, offbeats weakest.
Borrowed divisions substitute compound or simple divisions, creating triplets, duplets, and irregular divisions.
Resolve sevenths downward by step; exceptions for V 3 and retained sevenths allowed.
Interval structure determines chord quality: major, minor, diminished, or augmented triads.
Melody features include contour, conjunct/disjunct motion, register, range.
Sight-singing requires performing pitch, rhythm, and all expressive markings with steady tempo.
Harmonic rhythm is the rate of chord change, which can accelerate or decelerate within a passage.
Inverted triads require complete spelling of all chord tones.
Chords in tonal progressions function as tonic, dominant, or predominant based on contextual relationships.
Figured bass numerals indicate specific voice leading patterns in cadential 6/4.
Asymmetrical meters contain unequal beat divisions, contrasting with regular symmetrical meter patterns.
Omit fifth in root-position V7 and double root; inverted sevenths must be complete.
Passing 6/4 part-writing: double fifth, all voices move stepwise throughout.
Accurate melodic dictation requires identifying pitch relationships to tonic and correct notation.
Motive is recurring small musical idea developed through composition.
Vary outer voice motion; avoid more than three consecutive thirds or sixths.
Pedal 6/4 part-writing: static bass, upper voices resolve stepwise to next chord.
Vocal text setting is melismatic when syllable spans multiple pitches.
Leading tones resolve up; diminished fifths resolve inward to thirds naturally.
Chord tones distributed freely across pitch range while maintaining voice ranges.
Upper voices spaced max octave apart; bass can exceed octave to upper voices.
